Patch Set 4:

JC, if these patches are not directly dependent on each other then it is better to submit them from separate branches.  Otherwise, if there is any inspection issue with one of the patches it will require all of the later patches to also be resubmitted and retested and reinspected.  A patch series like this should only be used if the patches are directly dependent on each other (e.g. due to API changes, overlapping context in patches, functional requirements, etc).

Patch Set 15:

Putting the commcheck handler in ct_daemon() means that a failed comm check will not be propagated.

 # lhsmtool_posix --commcheck --noexecute lustre ; echo $?
0
 can't find fs root for '(null)': -19
 Can't open lustre: -19

 lhsmtool_posix: 18316: cannot start copytool interface: No such device

 # lhsmtool_posix --commcheck --hsm_root=/tmp/arc lustre; echo STATUS: $?
 STATUS: 0
 can't find fs root for '(null)': -19
 Can't open lustre: -19

 lhsmtool_posix: 18346: cannot start copytool interface: No  such device

Patch Set 15:

The newly added test failed with:

 /usr/lib64/lustre/tests/sanity-hsm.sh: line 204: /usr/lib64/lustre/utils/lhsmtool_posix: No such file or directory
 sanity-hsm test_11: @@@@@@ FAIL: import failed

Is lhsmtool_posix not added in the RPM package?

Patch Set 15:

--commcheck is an old option, not sure it is still needed. can we manage this in a further patch (copytool cleanning, with err messages)?

Patch Set 15:

About RPM issue, I am not using RPM for testing so I should have miss this, I will see how to add it.

Patch Set 15:

lhsmtool_posix is in the RPM at /usr/sbin/lhsmtool_posix. So the test error comes from the HT var init, Andreas do you know how the HT var is build by test framework ?

Patch Set 15:

You write that lhsmtool_posix is in /usr/sbin/lhsmtool_posix in the RPM, but according to the error message HT=/usr/lib64/lustre/utils/lhsmtool_posix.

Instead of supplying an absolute path for HT, it would be better to just add the directories to PATH and have the shell find where it is.

Patch Set 15:

Patch Set 17: I would prefer that you didn't submit this

(1 inline comment)

Looks like the newly-added test failed with:

 = sanity-hsm test 11: Import a file == 04:49:34 (1373629774)
 cannot truncate '/mnt/lustre/d0.sanity-hsm/d11/f.sanity-hsm.11' for import: No data available (61)
 lhsmtool_posix: '/mnt/lustre/d0.sanity-hsm/d11/f.sanity-hsm.11'
      import from '/tmp/arc/d0.sanity-hsm/d11/f.sanity-hsm.11' failed (No data available

Patch Set 17: (1 inline comment)

The failure comes from an incompatibility between test 229 and hsm import needs. When 229 was done it was decided to make truncate on released file failed. For HSM import we need to be able to set the size of a released file, and it is done with a truncate from user space, so no more work. I will make a new version of LU-3363 based on a iocontrol() which will do the import sequence from client kernel so no more need to use truncate call.

Patch Set 23:

Rosso Autotest has started the priority test process

Patch Set 23: (18 inline comments)

I think it's a defect not to hold open fds on the mount point and on .lustre/fid, both for ioctls() (this will require reworking the llapi hsm innterface slightly) and for lookups.

I'm also not happy about the fact that throughout HSM is using fsname to identify the filesystem, since it does not uniquely determine a mount point or even a filesystem on the client. At least we should take a mount point as the argument to lhsmtool_posix get it's fsname and then check that all the hals have the correct hal_fsname.

Can we create a struct ct_data which contains fds for the moint, and .lustre/fid, along with the mount point path, and fsname, and use that as our first argument rather than the "rootpath"? Since we only register as a CT once this struct can be initialized once in ct_daemon().

Several functions could be made static and several have a parameter spec of () where they should have (void).

Patch Set 23: Verified

Tests received by maloo, run on CentOS release 6.4/x86_64: (https://maloo.whamcloud.com/test_sessions/9f478e06-f68b-11e2-b7e5-52540035b04c).  Ran 17 tests.  No failures.

Patch Set 23: (9 inline comments)

We already have a patch which hide many things to CT (volatile creation, keep fd open ...) and implement a better agent interface. As it changes the llapi_, we were thinking to manage it as a following patch/enhancement of CT after the code freeze.
In this patch we will replace the use of FSNAME by mount point.
Uploaded patch set 24.

Patch Set 24:

Build Started http://build.whamcloud.com/job/lustre-reviews/17065/
Patch Set 23: (16 inline comments)

Patch Set 24:

Patchset #24 addresses most of the issues that John pointed out.

I've changed llapi_hsm_copytool_start() so that it opens a file descriptor on the mount point and the CT keeps it open until llapi_hsm_copytool_fini() is called.

We plan to make the other changes as part of the upcoming copytool userland API cleanup patch (LU-3600), on top of this one.

Uploaded patch set 26.

Patch Set 26:

Uploaded patchset #25 to address the concerns raised.

It also comes with an update of the copytool LLAPI, which we originally wanted to submit separately (LU-3600). According to the reviews, it seems that it's already needed.

Patch Set 26:

We changed the API a bit more than what you asked to make it simpler from the copytool side. It does now handle the volatile file creation/close for instance. This behavior is expected by the kernel, doing it in the LLAPI reduces the risk of error.

I might be missing your point, but llapi_hsm_action_begin() doesn't use mnt_fd because its the one initializing it. It could also take the copytool private structure and reuse its mnt_fd instead of taking the mount point and opening its own fd.

I can rename ct_restore_initialize to restore_intialize but this is clearly a LLAPI internal function.

Patch Set 26:

> I might be missing your point, but llapi_hsm_action_begin() doesn't use mnt_fd because its the one initializing it. It could also take the copytool private structure and reuse its mnt_fd instead of taking the
mount point and opening its own fd.

Right. Copytool registration returns to us a (struct hsm_copytool_private *hcp with the mnt_fd already set up. If this hcp is passed back to start a copy and we use:

int llapi_hsm_copy_start(struct hsm_copytool_private *hcp, struct hsm_copy *copy, const struct hsm_action_item *hai);

then the ioctl() can be performed on the mdt_fd already set up in hcp.

With respect to ct_restore_initialize() and wrapping the hsm_copy into hsm_copyaction_private I disagree. If we create the volatile file with O_LOV_DELAY_CREATE then there is no need for parent. We never cast a hsm_copyaction_private so there is no need for magic. We should not be using mnt, but just mnt fd, which we can get (internally) from hsm_copytool_private. data_fd can be managed by the CT without a dup(). Let's not make a big onion out of something that is supposed to be small and simple.

Patch Set 26:

Thanks for this nth review.

Let me elaborate on what we're trying to do. We noticed that handling the volatile file was a pain, and that it could easily be done from within the LLAPI. For that reason, the llapi_hsm_copyaction_*() functions need a private structure where to keep their FDs and everything.

If this is OK for you I'll change the llapi_hsm_action_begin() function to make it take a reference to the global copytool private structure instead of re-opening a FD on the mount point, but I'd still like to keep the hsm_copyaction_private structure.

As you understood, our plan was to submit it as a separate patch, but since you were already asking for changes that it contained I've merged both patches.

Concerning the location where to create the volatile files. We do now always create them w/o stripe, but I believe that not having them all created at the FS root scales better on DNE setups.

parents.subject: LU-3155 mgs: set_param -P option that sets value permanently

commit.subject: LU-2062 utils: HSM Posix CopyTool
commit.message:
LU-2062 utils: HSM Posix CopyTool

POSIX HSM CopyTool utils named lhsmtool_posix.
This user space command is the 'glue" between Lustre-HSM
and a POSIX filesytem used as a backend.
The main functionalities implemented are:
daemon mode:
- archive: read in lustre write with POSIX backend
- restore: read in POSIX backend write to Lustre
- remove: remove an entry from backend
cmd line mode:
- import: create in lustre a released file from a backend file
- rebind: change the FID associated to a file in the backend
- maxseq: get the larger sequence of FID found in the backend

The 2 last options are used for disaster recovery mode

This tools is also used for all the non regression tests made in
sanity-hsm.sh

Signed-off-by: JC Lafoucriere <jacques-charles.lafoucriere@cea.fr>
Signed-off-by: Henri Doreau <henri.doreau@cea.fr>
